blocks.text: Pinebrook Advisory Group LLC
Stephanie Campos, Chief Financial Officer
Johnson and Sons
Re: Operational Review
Dear Ms. Campos:

This letter sets out the terms on which Pinebrook Advisory Group LLC will conduct an operational review for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}, and it is meant to be read in full before it is signed. I keep a simple rule for anything that leaves this office: a client should be able to see the whole of an engagement on a single page and know exactly what we will do, who will do it, what it costs, and when it starts. What follows is that page.

The review covers your core operating functions: how work moves through the business, where time and cost accumulate, and which of those points will repay attention first. We spend our hours where the work actually happens rather than in a conference room. That means observing your operations on site, reading the records you already keep, and sitting with the people who run the day to day. You will not be handed a survey of everything that might be improved. You will be handed a short, ordered list of the things worth doing something about, each one concrete enough to act on.

Two of our consultants will carry the work. Jennifer Fletcher, our Senior Consultant, will lead the engagement and be your point of contact for how it is run; she holds the schedule and makes certain the numbers reconcile before anything is put in front of you. Robert Miller, our Principal Consultant, will spend the days on your floor, and the recommendations you read at the close will be grounded in what he sees there. I read everything before it reaches you and stand behind all of it.

Our fee for the review is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}}, which covers the work described above from start through delivery. There are no hourly charges to track and no reconciliation at the end. Any out-of-pocket costs, should they arise, would be modest and billed only as incurred, with documentation. We will begin the work on {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}} and expect the review to run about three months from that point.

If this reflects your understanding, please sign below and return one copy to my attention. If anything here reads differently than you expected, call me before you sign; a scope is far easier to settle now than in the middle of the work. We are glad to have the engagement and mean to give you reason to call us again.
Kickoff Memo: Operational Review for Johnson and Sons
To: Robert Miller, Michelle Lopez. From: Jennifer Fletcher. Re: Kickoff for the operational review, Johnson and Sons.

The engagement letter is signed and back, and we start fieldwork on {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}. Our job is to give {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} an honest, useful read on how its operations run: where the work bunches up, where cost and time leak out, and what is worth fixing first. Our contact is Stephanie Campos, their Chief Financial Officer, and anything going to her office goes out over my signature until I say otherwise. Let us keep this sequenced and not get ahead of the numbers.

items: Records and data: gather the process documentation, staffing lists, and volume figures the client is providing, and get them into working schedules before we are on site.
On-site observation: floor walk-throughs at each operating location, with layout, staffing, and every handoff noted on a common form. Robert Miller leads the days on the floor.
Interviews: sit down with the supervisors and the line staff who actually run the work, held at the client's offices so we see the setting.
Baseline: reduce what we collect to a plain cost-and-time picture we can defend, and from it a first short list of things worth changing.

blocks.text: Next steps are straightforward. Michelle Lopez will confirm the site-visit dates with Stephanie Campos's office this week and get the letterhead and engagement file in order. Robert drafts the observation form and I will draft the interview guide, both for us to review together before anyone is scheduled. Put any open questions in writing before we are on site. I would far rather answer them here than in front of the client.
Meeting Minutes: Operational Review Working Session

A working session on the operational review, held at the offices of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}. Minutes taken by Robert Miller and sent round to everyone present to correct anything I have set down wrong.

items: Robert Miller, Principal Consultant, Pinebrook Advisory Group LLC
Jennifer Fletcher, Senior Consultant, Pinebrook Advisory Group LLC
Stephanie Campos, Chief Financial Officer, Johnson and Sons

blocks.text: I walked the group through what the floor work has turned up so far. The observations at the main operation are done, and one pattern is already fairly plain: work stacks up at the handoff between order entry and the floor, and a good part of the day goes to waiting on that step rather than to the work itself. I was careful to call these observations and not conclusions. We hold the recommendations until the numbers stand behind them.

Stephanie Campos asked, fairly, whether what we are seeing is the busy season talking rather than the ordinary run of the business. It is the right question and we do not have the answer yet. Jennifer Fletcher noted that we need a clean twelve months of volume figures to tell the two apart, and until those reconcile we will not put a number on any of it. We agreed that nothing goes to the client's management as a finding before that check is finished.

rows: Pull the last twelve months of volume and staffing figures for the team
Finish the remaining floor observations at the second location
Reconcile the volume figures before any findings are drafted

blocks.text: Status Report: Operational Review for Johnson and Sons
Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} by Jennifer Fletcher, Senior Consultant, Pinebrook Advisory Group LLC.
Where Things Stand

The operational review is on schedule and in its final stretch. The fieldwork is finished, the findings are drafted, and we are now testing each recommendation against the data before it goes into the report. The work remains inside the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}} set in our engagement letter, and we see nothing at this point that would change that figure. If that expectation shifts, you will hear it from us before you read it anywhere else.
This Period

items: Completed the floor observations at both operating locations and the interviews with supervisors and line staff.
Reconciled the twelve-month volume and staffing figures; Robert Miller has the operating data tied out, and the findings now rest on numbers we can defend.
Drafted the findings and a first cut of the recommendations, ranked by what we would tackle first, and placed them in internal review.

blocks.text: Still to Come

items: Walk the draft findings through with your management and take their reaction before anything is called final.
Settle the last of the recommendations, keeping only those the data will carry.
Deliver the final report with the recommendations set out in priority order.

blocks.text: What Could Slow Us

Two things are worth flagging. A handful of the recommendations touch staffing and the flow of work, and changes of that kind take some lead time to put in place; we will mark each one so your team can plan around it. And a few data points are still being confirmed, though none large enough to move the overall picture. Neither of these threatens the schedule or the budget. Any question on this report can come to Robert Miller or to me.
